ADVERSARIAL CRITIQUE VERDICTS that shaped the final architecture (critiques) — (a) Next.js-as-executor is the most dangerous decision (HTTP/serverless timeout kills 10-30min runs); (b) LangGraph is overkill, plain asyncio dispatch table covers 90%, upgrade to Temporal only for durable crash-survival; (c) CanvasContext causes O(n) re-renders, use Zustand useStore selectors; (d) singular tokenBuffer breaks parallel streaming; (e) compression strategy has a summarization-loss positive-feedback-loop flaw; (f) SubtleCrypto-in-localStorage is security theater (XSS reads key+ciphertext).
deepInv.overallAssessment:
The file is a 350KB / 3690-line JSON capturing a multi-agent architectural analysis of the EARLIER SaaS-oriented 'Agent Workflow Organizer'. It has 11 top-level keys, but three are duplicates: critique_frontend/critique_backend/critique_orchestrator are byte-identical (hash-verified) to critiques.frontendCritique/backendCritique/orchestratorCritique — so there are effectively 8 unique content areas (frontend, backend, database, orchestrator, ux, roadmap, critiques, masterPlan). Content is dense and implementation-ready prose (not throwaway), with genuinely high-value verbatim artifacts.

The analysis contains INTERNAL CONTRADICTIONS that the masterPlan deliberately reconciles: the backend section picks Next.js+Bun (TypeScript), the orchestrator section is written entirely in Python/LangGraph/Pydantic/asyncio, and the critiques tear both down — then masterPlan settles on Hono (not Next.js), pure-TypeScript engine (no LangGraph), and Vercel AI SDK (no LiteLLM). The masterPlan is therefore the most decision-final layer and the closest match to the current product's architecture-decisions.md.

REUSABILITY against the current multi-surface local-first desktop vision (Tauri v2, SQLite+SQLCipher local / Postgres cloud Phase 2, OS Keychain, YAML workflow files, Vite+React+ReactFlow+Zustand, pure-TS engine, no Python, CLI + VS Code + desktop + portal surfaces):
- HIGHLY REUSABLE / language-agnostic: the orchestrator system prompt (8 sections) and agent-as-tool schema + description template; the workflow-definition JSON Schema (maps to .flowgent.yaml); the SQLite-vs-Postgres 14-difference table (directly serves the dual-DB plan); the RunEvent union + 150-line simplestOrchestrator blueprint (matches packages/core); the 10 failure modes; the design system + 24 shortcuts; the adversarial critique verdicts (several already baked into current decisions — ReactFlow-not-in-Context, no-LangGraph, server-side-keys).
- PARTIALLY REUSABLE (concepts yes, implementation must be re-grounded): the 13-table DB schema (rewrite Postgres types to SQLite/Drizzle, cost->micro-cents, drop multi-tenant/versioning bloat for MVP); the 22 REST endpoints (re-cast as local IPC/CLI commands for Phase 1, REST for Phase 2 portal); the frontend Zustand stores + SSE design (desktop uses the same engine but local execution may not need SSE-over-HTTP); the masterPlan tech stack (Vite/React/Hono/Vercel AI SDK/Drizzle/pure-TS engine all match; diverges on Postgres-first, server-side keys vs OS Keychain, BullMQ cloud-queue, single-surface vs four-surface).
- OUTDATED / pure-SaaS assumptions: BullMQ+Redis cloud queue and 3-worker-pool model (Phase 2 only), server-side AES-256-GCM key storage in Postgres (current vision uses OS Keychain + local-first, no central DB to decrypt), Better Auth + multi-tenant authz (current MVP needs no account), workflow_schedules/cron triggers (out of scope), MinIO/S3 artifact storage, the 16-week roadmap and build order (current order is engine -> CLI -> desktop -> VS Code -> cloud, and surfaces differ entirely). The LangGraph-specific tables (langgraph_checkpoints, langgraph_checkpoint_blobs) are obsolete given the no-LangGraph decision.

NET: This file is a rich quarry. Roughly 60-70% of the substance is preservable — the prompt-engineering artifacts, workflow schema, DB schema skeleton, SQLite/Postgres porting guide, engine event contract, failure-mode catalog, security checklist, design system, and critique verdicts should be documented. The cloud-infra/auth/queue/scheduling layers and the Next.js+LangGraph dead-ends should be archived as 'Phase 2 reference / rejected alternatives' rather than carried into current docs.

userConventions.fileNaming: kebab-case for topic docs (api-contract.md, local-dev-setup.md). ADRs use uppercase prefix + zero-padded number + slug (ADR-006-flutter-error-handling.md). Numbered-prefix files for ordered analysis series (01-genel-bakis.md ... 10-tasarim-ilkeleri.md). Reviews and one-off dated reports embed the date in the filename: reviews use full ISO timestamp (2026-03-16T00-32-41-review.md), root reports use date suffix (error-contract-analysis-2026-05-30.md). No spaces in filenames; Turkish chars avoided in filenames even though content is Turkish.
userConventions.markdownStyle: ATX headings starting at a single H1 title line 'Project — Doc Title'. Heavy, idiomatic table usage for any comparison/option/spec/metric (free-vs-premium, tech stack, error contract, KPIs, risks). Mermaid diagrams used liberally (graph/flowchart for architecture, gantt for roadmap, decision-tree flowcharts in indexes). Fenced code blocks are language-tagged (dart, sql, bash, yaml). Metadata carried as a top blockquote line under H1 (> Son güncelleme: / > Tarih: / > Kapsam:) rather than YAML front-matter. Blockquotes (>) used for notes, warnings (with ⚠️), and 'Kritik'/'Not' callouts. --- horizontal rules separate major sections. Task checklists (- [x]/- [ ]) used in roadmap acceptance criteria. Bold inline emphasis on key terms; occasional emoji as semantic markers (✅ done, ⚠️ warning, 🥇 winner). Prose is in Turkish; technical identifiers/paths/code stay in English. Code references cite file:line (Program.cs:119, dio_error_mapper.dart:76-80).
userConventions.adrStyle: MADR-like but lighter. Each ADR is a single file with H1 'ADR-NNN: Title', then a bold status/date header block (**Durum:** Kabul Edildi / **Tarih:** YYYY-MM-DD) separated by ---, then sections: Bağlam (Context), Karar (Decision, with the decision bolded), Gerekçe (Rationale, often a comparison table or code A-vs-B), Sonuçlar (Consequences), and frequently 'İlgili Dosyalar' (Related Files) and a forward-looking 'X Ne Zaman Eklenir?' (when to revisit) section. No 'Superseded by' chains observed yet. ADRs are numbered sequentially and never renumbered; a README.md index table lists all ADRs and documents the two-repo numbering split.
userConventions.datingConvention: ISO-8601 (YYYY-MM-DD) everywhere in content metadata and filenames; review filenames use ISO datetime with colons replaced by hyphens (2026-03-16T00-32-41). One review used a long-form Turkish date in body ('17 Mart 2026') but ISO is the dominant/preferred form. Dates surfaced via top blockquote ('> Tarih:' / '> Son güncelleme:' / '> Analiz tarihi:') and via dated filenames for snapshots.

bestPractices.practice: Diátaxis framework: split user-facing docs along the two axes (theory vs practice, study vs work) into four distinct modes — Tutorials (learning-oriented, guided), How-to guides (task-oriented, problem-solving), Reference (information-oriented, dry/complete), Explanation (understanding-oriented, discursive). Never mix a tutorial step into a reference table or bury a 'why' inside a how-to.
bestPractices.applicability: High for a multi-surface dev tool with external users (CLI, VS Code ext, web portal each need their own quickstart tutorial + how-tos + reference). Maps cleanly onto the user's existing split: runbooks/ already behaves like Diátaxis how-to; architecture/ explanation overlaps with Diátaxis explanation; a reference/ folder for CLI flags / SDK API / config schema is currently missing and worth adding per surface.

bestPractices.practice: MADR (Markdown Architecture Decision Records) template: one file per decision, status + date metadata, sections Context and Problem Statement, Decision Drivers, Considered Options, Decision Outcome (with positive/negative consequences), optional Pros/Cons per option and More Information/links. Keep ADRs immutable; supersede rather than edit, recording 'Superseded by ADR-NNN'.
bestPractices.applicability: High and nearly already adopted — the user's ADR format is a lighter MADR variant. Recommend keeping their lighter shape but formally adding a Status lifecycle (Proposed/Accepted/Deprecated/Superseded) and a 'Considered Options' section name, since a 4-surface product will generate many cross-surface trade-off decisions that benefit from explicit option comparison and supersession chains.

bestPractices.practice: C4 model for architecture: describe the system at four zoom levels — Context (system + users + external systems), Container (deployable/runnable units: apps, services, datastores), Component (major building blocks inside a container), and Code (optional, usually class diagrams). Diagram-per-level, each with a short narrative; keep Context/Container hand-maintained and Code generated.
bestPractices.applicability: High for a multi-surface product. The user's architecture/overview.md is effectively a C4 Container diagram already (Mermaid). Recommend making the C4 levels explicit: one Context diagram for the whole platform, one Container diagram per surface (desktop app, VS Code ext, CLI, web portal) plus shared core, and Component diagrams only where complexity warrants. Mermaid (already in use) renders C4 well.

bestPractices.practice: Per-surface architecture documentation in large dev-tool monorepos: give each surface/package its own scoped doc set (often a README/docs at the package root) while keeping one top-level cross-cutting set for shared concerns (build, release, shared core, contracts). Use a docs landing/index page and explicit cross-links; document the public contract (API/protocol/schema) once as the source of truth and link surfaces to it.
bestPractices.applicability: High. The product is explicitly a TypeScript monorepo with 4 surfaces over a shared core. Recommend an architecture/surfaces/ subfolder (one file per surface) plus shared docs at architecture/ root, and a single contract reference (the AI-SDK/agent-workflow schema, IPC, and persistence schema) that all surfaces link to — mirroring how the user already centralizes api-contract.md and database-schema.md.

bestPractices.practice: Maintain a docs landing/index page and a decisions index (README) so the doc set is navigable; record 'last updated' on living docs and treat dated snapshots (reviews, analyses) as immutable point-in-time artifacts.
bestPractices.applicability: High — the user already does this (decisions/README.md index, '> Son güncelleme' on roadmap, dated review/analysis filenames). Extend the same pattern with a top-level docs/README.md index landing page, which is currently absent.

bestPractices.practice: Keep reference material complete and machine-checkable where possible (generate CLI/SDK/config reference from source; validate code samples in CI) so reference docs do not drift from the product.
bestPractices.applicability: Medium-to-high for the CLI and SDK surfaces specifically; lower for explanation/ADR content. Aligns with the user's habit of citing file:line and exact endpoints/JSON, which signals a strong preference for precision over hand-waving.

recommendedConventions: Adopt the user's existing six-folder taxonomy verbatim as the backbone: analysis/, architecture/, decisions/, runbooks/, ideas/, reviews/ — it already mirrors Diátaxis-explanation + how-to and MADR. Add only what is missing for an external-facing dev tool.

Add a reference/ folder (currently absent) for dry, complete, machine-checkable reference material: CLI command/flag reference, SDK/API reference, config-schema reference, and the agent-workflow file format — one sub-area per surface. This fills the one Diátaxis quadrant the user does not yet cover.

Add a tutorials/ (or guides/getting-started) area for learning-oriented onboarding per surface (desktop app, VS Code ext, CLI, web portal); keep the user's existing runbooks/ as the task-oriented how-to bucket. This makes the Diátaxis split explicit without renaming what already works.

Keep the user's lighter MADR-style ADRs (H1 'ADR-NNN: Title' + bold Durum/Tarih + Bağlam/Karar/Gerekçe/Sonuçlar + İlgili Dosyalar), but formalize a Status lifecycle (Proposed → Accepted → Deprecated/Superseded) and add an explicit 'Considered Options' section plus 'Superseded by ADR-NNN' links, since a 4-surface platform will produce cross-surface trade-offs. Continue the never-renumber rule and the per-repo ADR-number-space convention, with a decisions/README.md index.

Make the C4 levels explicit in architecture/: one platform-wide Context diagram, a Container diagram per surface plus shared core, Component diagrams only where warranted — all in Mermaid (already the user's diagramming tool). Treat overview.md as the Context/Container entry point.

Document per-surface architecture under architecture/surfaces/<surface>.md while keeping cross-cutting concerns (shared core, build/release, persistence) at architecture/ root, and centralize the cross-surface contract(s) as a single source of truth that every surface links to — extending the user's existing api-contract.md / database-schema.md pattern.

Follow the user's markdown conventions exactly: single H1 'Project — Title', a top blockquote metadata line (> Son güncelleme: YYYY-MM-DD / > Tarih:) instead of YAML front-matter, heavy table usage for any comparison/spec/metric, Mermaid diagrams, language-tagged fenced code, > blockquote callouts with ⚠️ for warnings, --- section rules, and file:line citations for code references.

Use ISO-8601 dates everywhere; encode snapshot dates into filenames (reviews: full ISO timestamp with hyphens; one-off reports: -YYYY-MM-DD suffix). Treat dated files in reviews/ and analysis/ as immutable; carry a '> Son güncelleme' line on living docs in architecture/ and roadmap.md.

Use kebab-case for all topic filenames, numbered NN- prefixes for ordered series in analysis/ (and any new multi-part tutorial), and avoid spaces and Turkish-specific characters in filenames (content language can remain whatever the user writes; identifiers/paths/code stay in English).

Add a top-level docs/README.md landing/index page (currently missing) that links the taxonomy and key entry points (roadmap, architecture overview, ADR index, per-surface reference), so the growing multi-surface doc set stays navigable.

finalStructure.philosophy: Backbone = the user's REAL surveyed cross-project house style (memory/docs-house-style.md), NOT a numbered hierarchy. The user's docs DO NOT use numbered top-level folders (Candidate A's premise is wrong against the actual Saydın repo and the house-style memo): they use named folders + flat product docs at root + per-subfolder README index + kebab-case ADRs NNNN-kebab.md + H1-with-bold-metadata (no YAML front-matter) + a static marketing landing page living in docs/ (css/, js/, index.html — confirmed present in the real Saydın repo, which validates Candidate B's marketing observation). So the structure adopts that backbone verbatim and adds ONLY the two things the user provably lacks and an external dev tool needs: (1) Diátaxis completeness — a reference/ quadrant (CLI/API/config/file-format) and a tutorials/ quadrant (learning-oriented onboarding), keeping the user's existing runbooks/ as the how-to bucket; (2) a surface axis nested INSIDE the type axis (reference/desktop, reference/cli, reference/vscode, tutorials/desktop, etc.) — this is Candidate B's genuinely strong contribution, the multi-surface product cannot be documented on one axis alone. From Candidate A I keep two structurally important ideas that scale the tree: the 'two ADR number spaces' routing README (one space for this meta repo, a reserved second space for the Phase-2 backend repo, so nothing renumbers later) and the 'preserve the old deep-analysis verbatim, never edit it' archive principle under analysis/. The dual-vision tension (older partially-reusable frontend deep-analysis vs current authoritative desktop/Tauri pivot) is resolved structurally: the current authoritative vision drives every LIVING doc (architecture/, reference/, explanation/), while the prior deep-analysis JSON is frozen verbatim under analysis/_archive/ and only its still-true pieces are cited (never copied) into living docs. Every concrete artifact from the inventory (workflow YAML spec, agent YAML, SSE schema, IPC contract, SQLite DDL, 5 Zustand store shapes, 9 node types, 12 built-in tools, MCP pool, config.toml) lands in exactly ONE canonical reference home and is cross-linked, never duplicated. Result: feels 100% native to the user's other 8 projects on day one, yet is complete enough to ship as public dev-tool documentation.

namingConventions.files: kebab-case, English. Living topic docs: kebab-case.md (e.g. execution-model.md). ADRs: NNNN-kebab-title.md with zero-padded 4-digit number (e.g. 0007-desktop-is-not-an-ide.md) — note this UPGRADES Saydin's ADR-NNN- to the house-style memo's 4-digit NNNN-, the user's documented current standard. Reviews: ISO timestamp YYYY-MM-DDTHH-MM-SS-<slug>-review.md (mirrors Saydin reviews/). Dated analysis: <name>-YYYY-MM-DD.md. Frozen raw inputs prefixed and suffixed clearly: deep-analysis-raw.json / synthesis-raw.json under analysis/_archive/. Surface sub-areas are bare lowercase folder names: desktop/ cli/ vscode/ portal/ shared-core/ contracts/. No spaces, no uppercase except the literal README.md / ADR file convention.
namingConventions.frontMatter: NONE. The user's real repos and the house-style memo use NO YAML front-matter in docs/ files. Instead every doc opens with a single H1 title, optionally followed by bold metadata lines on their own lines: e.g. '# ADR-0007: Desktop is not an IDE' then '- **Status**: Accepted' / '- **Date**: 2026-06-03' / '- **Related**: [product-constraints.md](../product-constraints.md)'. Living docs that track freshness use a blockquote '> Last updated: YYYY-MM-DD' just under the H1 (mirrors Saydin roadmap.md '> Son guncelleme:'). Do NOT add Jekyll/front-matter blocks.
namingConventions.headings: Sentence-case H1 that is the document title (project-prefixed only where Saydin does, e.g. ADR ids). Use ## for major sections, ### for sub. ADRs use the fixed section set: ## Context, ## Decision, ## Consequences with ### Positive / ### Negative (English MADR, since this is an external dev tool — Saydin's Turkish Baglam/Karar/Sonuclar headings are localized; Flowgent docs are English). Architecture docs lead with a mermaid diagram block right after the H1 where a topology exists (matches Saydin architecture/overview.md).
namingConventions.dating: ISO 8601 YYYY-MM-DD everywhere. Filenames suffix the date for point-in-time analysis/reviews; living docs carry '> Last updated:' inside. Today is 2026-06-03 for any seeded dates.

styleGuideSummary: No YAML front-matter. Every file starts with one H1 title; metadata goes in bold key lines (**Status**, **Date**, **Related**) or a '> Last updated:' blockquote.

Type axis is the top-level folder (explanation lives in architecture/, reference is dry contracts, tutorials are learning, runbooks are how-to, decisions are ADRs, standards are binding rules). Surface axis (desktop/cli/vscode/portal/shared-core) is nested INSIDE reference/ and tutorials/ only — never a top-level folder.

One canonical home per artifact. Every concrete spec (workflow YAML, agent YAML, SSE schema, IPC contract, config, store shapes, node types, tools, MCP, DDL, routes) lives in exactly one reference/ file. Architecture/explanation/tutorials CITE it by relative link, never copy it.
Every subfolder has a README.md index listing its files with one-line purposes (house-style invariant).

ADRs use 4-digit NNNN-kebab-title.md, English MADR sections (Context / Decision / Consequences->Positive/Negative), cross-link sibling ADRs, and reference tech-stack.md for pinned versions instead of restating them.

Two ADR number spaces: this meta/product repo owns 0001+; the Phase-2 flowgent-cloud repo will own its own independent space. Collisions across repos are expected and fine — never renumber. decisions/README.md is the routing table.

Current authoritative vision (Tauri desktop pivot, synthesis JSON) drives ALL living docs. The prior deep-analysis JSON is frozen verbatim in analysis/_archive/ and only its still-true pieces are cited, never edited or copied wholesale.
Mark Phase-2 content explicitly (cloud-phase-2.md, portal/api-reference.md, etc.) so readers never mistake it for shipped behavior.
English, kebab-case filenames, ISO dates, tables used liberally, mermaid for topology — exactly as the user's other 8 repos.
Product docs stay flat at docs/ root (vision.md, product-constraints.md, uvp.md, tech-stack.md, project-structure.md, roadmap.md, glossary.md) — do not bury them in subfolders.
finalStructure.crossLinkingStrategy: "All links are RELATIVE markdown paths (e.g. [tech-stack.md](../tech-stack.md), [workflow YAML spec](../reference/contracts/workflow-yaml-spec.md)) so the tree is portable and renders on GitHub — matches Saydin's [ADR-001](../decisions/ADR-001-no-kafka-mvp.md) style. Hub-and-spoke: docs/README.md is the single entry hub linking every top-level area via the Document Map; each subfolder README.md is a secondary hub for its files. Directionality rules: (1) explanation/architecture docs link DOWN to the canonical reference/ spec for any artifact they mention, never restate it; (2) ADRs link to tech-stack.md for versions and to sibling/superseding ADRs ('Superseded by [ADR-00XX]'); (3) reference specs link UP to the architecture doc that gives their rationale and ACROSS to related specs (sse-event-schema <-> ipc-contract, store-shapes <-> state-management); (4) tutorials and runbooks link to the reference they operationalize; (5) glossary terms are the vocabulary every doc uses, and major docs link first-use of a term back to glossary.md. The analysis/_archive/README.md is a provenance map: it links each frozen JSON section to the living doc it seeded (one-way, archive -> living). No absolute URLs for internal docs; external URLs (provider docs, Tauri, Vercel AI SDK) allowed inline. Avoid deep link chains — prefer linking to a doc's H1 or a stable ## anchor."
finalStructure.rawFilesHandling: "Both raw JSON analysis files are preserved VERBATIM and committed under docs/analysis/_archive/ as immutable provenance artifacts — never edited after landing. (1) The prior partially-reusable deep-analysis (frontend/backend/database/orchestrator/ux/masterPlan/critiques) -> docs/analysis/_archive/deep-analysis-raw.json. (2) The current authoritative synthesis/pivot (productIdentity, UVP, workflowYAMLSpec v1.0, corePortal contracts) -> docs/analysis/_archive/synthesis-raw.json. A docs/analysis/_archive/README.md acts as the provenance index, mapping each JSON section to the living doc(s) it seeded (e.g. synthesis.corePortal.sharedCore.workflowYAMLSpec -> reference/contracts/workflow-yaml-spec.md; deepAnalysis.frontend -> architecture/state-management.md & architecture/desktop-architecture.md as cited background). The _archive prefix sorts it to the top of analysis/ and signals 'frozen, do not edit'. Living docs cite these files for provenance but never embed them; when the two sources conflict, the synthesis (current vision) wins for living docs and the deep-analysis is treated as historical context only. This mirrors Saydin's analysis/<subject>/ never-edited research archive while keeping the raw inputs auditable."

populationOrder.rationale: Land the two frozen raw inputs FIRST so every later doc has an auditable, linkable source of truth and the provenance map exists before anything cites it. Pure copy/commit, zero authoring risk.

Write the rules before the content so every subsequent doc is born compliant (no-front-matter, naming, axes, cross-linking, archive rule). The style guide is item #4 and the contract all other files follow.
These are 'ready' (verbatim from memory) — fastest wins, and they are the anchors ADRs and architecture docs link back to for scope and pinned versions.

Mostly 'ready' from architecture-decisions.md + product-constraints.md. Capturing decisions early freezes rationale and gives architecture/ docs stable ADR links. Routing README first establishes the two-space scheme.

populationOrder.rationale: The reference quadrant is where the most 'ready' concrete artifacts live (YAML spec v1.0, SSE, IPC, config, store-shapes, node-types, tools, MCP, DDL, routes). Build the canonical homes before any doc tries to cite them, preventing duplication.

Architecture/explanation docs cite reference/ specs and ADRs, so they come after both exist. overview.md (ready) anchors the rest; surface docs are 'partial' and pull cited background from the frozen deep-analysis.
Cross-cutting product docs that reference terminology and artifacts defined above; glossary is easiest once node/trigger/tool vocab is settled in reference/.
The root index Document Map + Reading Order can only be accurate once the tree is populated; write it after the structure is real so links don't dangle.

Task- and learning-oriented docs operationalize the now-existing reference/ and architecture/. Mostly to-be-written; lowest urgency for an internal-first Phase 1 and best authored once the product surfaces stabilize.

Idea notes and the empty reviews bucket are ongoing/as-needed; the marketing landing page (index.html/css/js) is presentation-only and best done last, near a public launch, reusing productIdentity copy.
